Research Working Group — Steering Committee

Rush University Medical Center
Data: OpenAlex · May 2026
n = 1,688 faculty
Overview

Faculty Directory

How to Read This Dashboard

  • H-Index — A researcher with h-index h has published at least h papers, each cited at least h times. It captures sustained impact over a career, not just volume.
  • Rush 5yr — Publications from 2021–2026 where at least one author lists a Rush affiliation. This is the best available measure of recent institutional research activity.
  • Senior Author 5yr — Publications from 2021–2026 where the faculty member appears as last author in OpenAlex. This is a proxy for likely research ownership or senior leadership, not a judgment about authorship appropriateness.
  • 2yr Citedness — Average citations per paper published in the last two years. Captures how much attention a researcher’s recent work is attracting from the field.
  • Important: H-indices are not comparable across disciplines. An h-index of 15 in nursing represents a different level of achievement than h = 15 in molecular biology. Any cross-department comparison should account for field-specific citation norms.
  • Data source: Faculty names from the Rush University directory (rushu.rush.edu). Publication metrics from OpenAlex, an open scholarly database. Some faculty may be unmatched or misattributed—see Methodology on the Faculty Directory tab for details.
Most Rush Faculty Have Published Fewer Than 10 Papers—but a Small Group Has Exceptional Impact
1,688 faculty grouped by career impact tier
How to read this: Each bar shows how many faculty fall in an h-index range. An h-index of 20 means at least 20 papers cited 20+ times each. The heavy left skew is typical of clinical-focused academic medical centers where most faculty prioritize patient care. Faculty with h = 0 may be primarily clinical, early career, or not yet indexed in OpenAlex.
Source: OpenAlex (openalex.org) · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Rush Medical College Faculty Publish Substantially More Than Nursing or Health Sciences
H-index distribution among faculty with at least one publication, by college
How to read this: Each box spans the middle 50% of faculty; the line inside is the median. Dots beyond the whiskers are outliers. The gap between colleges reflects disciplinary publishing norms—citation rates vary by field—not research quality. An h-index of 15 in nursing and h = 15 in molecular biology represent very different levels of achievement.
Source: OpenAlex · May 2026
No Department Leads on Every Metric—Each Has a Distinct Research Profile
Departments sorted by composite score. Darker green = stronger relative performance. Each cell shows the actual value.
How to read this: Each row is a department; each column is a research metric. Darker green means a department ranks higher among Rush departments on that metric; white means lower. “Research Active %” is the share of faculty with any publications. “Research Equity” (1 − Gini) measures how evenly output is distributed—higher means research is spread across more faculty rather than concentrated in a few stars.
Source: OpenAlex · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
A Handful of Departments Stand Out for Both Research Depth and Breadth
Horizontal = Research Active % (breadth) · Vertical = Median H-Index among publishing faculty (depth) · Bubble size = faculty count · Dashed lines at institutional medians
How to read this: Upper-right (“Research Culture”): both high participation and high individual impact. Upper-left (“Star-Dependent”): a few high-impact individuals but limited broad participation. Lower-right (“Emerging”): many faculty are publishing, but at lower impact levels so far. Lower-left (“Teaching-Focused”): lower participation and impact—which may be entirely appropriate for a department’s clinical or teaching mission. Bubble colors: green = Rush Medical College, teal = Health Sciences, light green = Nursing.
Source: OpenAlex · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Department Rankings by Research Metrics
Click any column header to re-sort. Green = top quartile, yellow = middle, red = bottom quartile. Rankings reflect bibliometric indicators only—interpret alongside each department’s mission and field norms.
Rank ▼ Department Faculty Active% Median H Max H Med Rush 5yr Total Cites Gini
Compare Any Departments Side by Side
Select 2–4 departments to overlay their profiles. Each axis is normalized 0–100 relative to the top department on that metric.
How to read this: Each spoke is a research metric, scaled so 100 = the top department. A larger, more even shape means balanced strength across dimensions. A spike on one axis with dips elsewhere signals a lopsided profile—strong in one area but with gaps.
Source: OpenAlex · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Most Departments Rely on Emerging Researchers—Few Have Deep Benches of Established Scholars
Faculty broken into career tiers by h-index. Sorted by share in Established+ tiers (h ≥ 20).
How to read this: Each bar shows one department’s faculty across six tiers: No Publications (gray, h=0), Emerging (light green, h=1–9), Developing (medium green, h=10–19), Established (green, h=20–39), Research Leader (teal, h=40–59), and Eminent Scholar (dark teal, h≥60). Longer green and teal segments mean a deeper bench. Departments near the top have more senior research capacity. These tiers are approximate—the same h-index range reflects different achievement levels across disciplines.
Source: OpenAlex · May 2026
In Most Departments, a Handful of Faculty Produce the Majority of Research
Select a department. The diagonal line = perfectly equal distribution.
How to read this: The green curve traces cumulative publications from least to most productive faculty. If output were perfectly equal, the curve would follow the diagonal. The more it bows downward, the more concentrated output is in a few people. The Gini coefficient quantifies this: 0 = equal, 1 = one person produces everything. Most academic departments fall between 0.4 and 0.8.
Where Should Rush Invest? Departments With High Momentum but Low Current Output Represent Growth Opportunities
Horizontal = current output (median h-index + participation rate). Vertical = recent momentum (Rush 5yr + citation velocity). Dashed lines at the midpoint.
How to read this: Sustain & Scale (upper-right): high output and strong momentum—research leaders. Invest Now (lower-right): strong legacy output but slowing momentum—may need targeted investment. At Risk (upper-left): gaining momentum from a low base—emerging programs worth watching. Mission Review (lower-left): low on both axes—may be primarily clinical or teaching-focused, which is a legitimate mission. Bubble size = faculty count.
Senior Researchers Don’t Always Have the Most Recent Output—and Vice Versa
Each dot is one faculty member. Horizontal = lifetime h-index. Vertical = Rush-affiliated publications in the last 5 years. Use the dropdown to filter by department.
How to read this: Upper-right: active senior researchers with both high career impact and strong recent output. Upper-left: publishing actively at Rush but earlier in their careers. Lower-right: established reputations but fewer recent Rush-affiliated publications—may be nearing retirement, shifted roles, or publishing through other affiliations. Hover over any dot for details.
Source: OpenAlex · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
The 20 Most Prolific Rush Researchers in the Last Five Years
Faculty ranked by Rush-affiliated publications, 2021–2026
How to read this: Each bar is one faculty member’s Rush-affiliated publication count over the last five years. High counts may reflect large collaborative programs, prolific individual output, or both. The department label shows which programs drive the most recent output.
Source: OpenAlex · Rush faculty directory · May 2026

How to Read the Leadership Lens

  • Last authorship is used as the primary senior-author signal. In many biomedical fields, this often reflects lab, program, or study leadership, but conventions vary by field and paper type.
  • First authorship is available as an emerging-leadership signal, especially for early-career faculty, trainees, and faculty building independent scholarly identity. It is not interchangeable with last authorship.
  • Middle authorship is labeled as contributing authorship here. It can represent important collaboration, samples, methods, recruitment, or analytic support, but it is a different signal than first- or last-author leadership.
  • External contributor means a middle-author work where no Rush affiliation was detected anywhere in the OpenAlex authorship data. This is useful for spotting externally led collaborations, not for discounting legitimate contributions.
  • Denominator: These metrics count faculty-authorship records, not unique papers. If two Rush faculty appear on the same paper, each faculty-paper attribution is counted once.
Ranked view
Last-author view emphasizes senior ownership and program leadership. Use first-author view to surface early-career and hands-on scholarly leadership.
Authorship Role Mix Separates Last-Author, First-Author, and Middle-Author Patterns
Each bar sums to 100% of recent matched faculty-authorship records, 2021–2026. Sorted by last-author share; departments with no matched recent records are omitted.
How to read this: Green segments show last-author records, a proxy for senior leadership in many biomedical fields. Teal shows first-author records, gray shows middle-author contribution, and pale gray captures single-author or ambiguous records. Use the n= labels to judge stability: small departments can swing substantially with only a few papers.
Source: OpenAlex authorship-position data · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Top Faculty by Senior-Author Publications
Last-author publications from 2021–2026, with Rush-affiliated senior works shown in green
How to read this: These are faculty whose recent publication records most strongly show the senior-author pattern. The darker segment is senior-authored work with Rush detected in the work’s affiliation data.
Source: OpenAlex authorship-position data · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Departments Differ in Senior-Led vs Contributor-Led Output
Horizontal = total recent faculty works. Vertical = share that are last-author works.
How to read this: Higher departments have a larger share of recent works where Rush faculty are last authors. Far-right departments have higher recent publication volume overall. Bubble size reflects faculty count.
Source: OpenAlex authorship-position data · May 2026
Department Rank Changes Under the Senior-Author Lens
Compare current Rush-affiliated publication volume rank with rank by senior-author publications.
Senior Rank ▼ Department Rush Volume Rank Rank Change Senior 5yr Rush Senior 5yr Recent Works Senior Share External Contributor
How to read this: Positive rank change means a department ranks higher when publications are weighted by last authorship than by raw Rush-affiliated volume. Negative change means high publication volume is more often driven by middle-author collaboration or non-senior roles.

How to Read Strategy Metrics

  • Field-normalized impact uses OpenAlex field-weighted citation impact and top-10% citation percentile flags. This is better for cross-field strategy than raw citations, but it is still a bibliometric proxy.
  • Topic portfolio uses each work’s OpenAlex primary topic. Topics are useful for finding research concentration and growth opportunities, not for assigning departmental ownership.
  • Rush-led team science counts cross-department works where a Rush faculty member appears as first, last, or single author. It is a leadership signal, not proof of who controlled the study.
  • NIH funding linkage uses Rush University Medical Center projects from NIH RePORTER, conservatively matched to faculty by unique first-name and last-name match. Unmatched and ambiguous PI rows remain in the audit file.
Field-Normalized Impact Highlights Departments Punching Above Raw Volume
Departments sorted by share of unique recent works in the top 10% for field-normalized citation impact.
How to read this: Each bar shows the department share of recent unique works that OpenAlex flags in the top 10% for field-normalized citation impact. Labels show mean field-weighted citation impact. Use this to identify areas with high influence after adjusting for field differences.
Source: OpenAlex field-normalized citation metrics · Rush faculty-work audit · May 2026
Research Topic Portfolio Shows Scale, Impact, and Rush-Led Presence
Top OpenAlex topics by Rush work count. Bubble size = Rush-led works.
How to read this: Farther right means more recent Rush work in a topic; higher means higher mean field-weighted citation impact. Larger bubbles show topics where Rush faculty more often appear in first, last, or single-author positions.
Source: OpenAlex primary topics and field-weighted citation impact · May 2026
Rush-Led Cross-Department Team Science
Cross-department publications with Rush first-, last-, or single-author leadership signal.
How to read this: This favors internal collaboration that also shows a Rush leadership position. It helps identify departments that are not only collaborating but helping lead multi-department work.
Source: OpenAlex authorship positions · Rush faculty-work audit · May 2026
NIH Funding Linkage by Department
Rush University Medical Center NIH RePORTER projects, 2021–2026, matched to faculty PI names when unique.
How to read this: Bars show total NIH project-year cost among conservatively matched Rush faculty PIs. Markers show active project counts. Because matching is by faculty name, use this for strategy and audit follow-up, not as a final grants ledger.
Source: NIH RePORTER API · Rush faculty directory · May 2026
Strategy Dashboard: Topic and Funding Signals by Department
Click any column header to sort. Funding is NIH RePORTER only and excludes unmatched or ambiguous PI rows from faculty-level attribution.
